title: The fixer part that cannot stop rescuing other people
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The one who never rests

I've been the fixer my whole life. The one who scans a room for the person in pain. The one who feels a physical pull toward anyone struggling. The one who cannot rest while someone else is hurting. And honestly? I thought this was my best quality. I thought being the rescuer was proof that I was good. That I cared. That I was different from the people who walked past suffering without a second glance.

But here's the thing. That part of me that rushes in to save everyone? It's exhausted. It's been running on empty for decades. And it's not actually helping anyone the way I thought it was.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems therapy, would call this part a "manager" or a "protector." It's a part that developed early, probably when you were a child who had to take care of adults who couldn't take care of themselves. Maybe you soothed a parent's emotional storms. Maybe you parented younger siblings. Maybe you learned that your value came from what you could do for others, not from who you were.

That part is still running the show. And it's running you into the ground.

Think about it. When was the last time you spent an entire day without trying to fix someone? Without offering advice to a friend who didn't ask? Without taking on someone else's problem as your own? Without feeling responsible for another adult's feelings?

If you're like me, that day was a long time ago. Maybe never.

Why the fixer part exists

Let me tell you something I learned the hard way. The fixer part didn't appear out of nowhere. It was born in survival. Peter Levine, the developer of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how our nervous systems adapt to threat. When you grew up in an environment where love was conditional, where you had to earn care by being useful, your brain made a calculation: "If I can fix the people around me, maybe they'll be safe. Maybe they'll see me. Maybe they'll love me."

That's not weakness. That's genius. Your younger self figured out a strategy that kept you alive. That part deserves gratitude, not criticism.

But here's the problem. That strategy worked when you were small and powerless. Now you're an adult with resources and choices. And that part is still running the same script, unaware that the danger has passed. It's like a smoke alarm that keeps going off because it's still calibrated for a house fire that happened thirty years ago.

You don't need to rescue everyone anymore. You don't need to earn your place in the world by bleeding yourself dry for others. You don't need to be useful to be worthy of love.

I know that sentence might make your chest tighten. I know the part that believes "if I stop fixing, I'll be abandoned" is screaming right now. Stay with me here.

The fixer part is terrified of what will happen if it stops. It believes that your relationships are held together by the thread of your usefulness. It believes that if you stop solving problems, people will leave. It believes that your worth is transactional, that love is a reward for service.

And that belief? It came from somewhere real. Someone taught you that. Probably by how they treated you, not by what they said. Your nervous system learned that safety required performance. That's not your fault. But it is your responsibility to question now.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). It helped me understand that every part of me, including the exhausted fixer, has good intentions. It's just that the strategy is outdated.

The cost of constant rescuing

I want to be honest with you about what this pattern costs. Because I think you know, but you might not have let yourself feel it.

There's the obvious cost: burnout. Exhaustion. Resentment. The way you snap at people you love because you've given so much that there's nothing left for yourself. The way your body holds tension in your shoulders, your jaw, your lower back. The way you wake up tired even after eight hours of sleep.

But there's a deeper cost too. One that's harder to see.

When you're always fixing, you're not actually present. You're not sitting with someone in their pain. You're jumping ahead to solutions. You're trying to make the discomfort go away because their discomfort triggers your discomfort. Right? You're not really listening. You're strategizing. You're problem-solving. You're doing the thing that makes you feel useful so you don't have to feel helpless.

Janina Fisher, who writes extensively about trauma and parts work, would say that the fixer is a protector part that keeps you from feeling the vulnerability underneath. The helplessness. The powerlessness. The grief of not being able to fix your own childhood.

When you rescue someone, you get to feel powerful. You get to feel in control. You get to avoid the unbearable feeling of sitting with someone's pain and not being able to make it better. But here's the truth: some pain can't be fixed. It can only be witnessed. It can only be held. And that requires a different kind of strength.

The fixer part doesn't know how to do that. It only knows how to do. It doesn't know how to be.

And here's another cost: the people you're rescuing aren't actually being helped. When you solve someone's problems for them, you're robbing them of their own agency. You're sending a message that they can't handle their own life. You're creating dependency, not empowerment. I know that's hard to hear. I know your fixer part is defensive right now, coming up with a dozen reasons why this situation is different. But sit with it anyway.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on the fawn response helped me see how my rescuing was actually a survival strategy, not a sign of generosity.

What to do with the fixer part

So what do we do with this part? We don't get rid of it. We can't. And honestly, we shouldn't want to. This part has kept you alive. It has made you compassionate. It has driven you to help people who genuinely needed help. The problem isn't the part itself. The problem is that it's running the whole show without any input from the rest of you.

What the fixer part needs is not exile. It needs a new job description.

Start by noticing when the fixer takes over. Notice the physical sensations. For me, it's a tightness in my chest and a racing mind that won't stop generating solutions. For you, it might be something different. Maybe your shoulders go up. Maybe your breathing gets shallow. Maybe you feel a compulsive urge to speak, to offer advice, to do something.

Just notice. Don't judge. Don't try to change it yet. Notice what just happened in your body.

Then try something radical. Ask the fixer part what it's afraid would happen if it stopped. Really listen. Don't argue with the answer. Just let the part speak. It might say, "People will leave." It might say, "I'll be worthless." It might say, "The pain will be unbearable." Whatever it says, thank it. It's been working so hard for so long.

Then try this: instead of fixing, try witnessing. When someone tells you about their pain, resist the urge to offer solutions. Say something like, "That sounds really hard. I'm here with you." That's it. No advice. No fixing. No trying to make it better. Just presence.

Notice how uncomfortable that is. Notice how your body wants to DO something. That discomfort is the fixer part screaming. And that's okay. You can let it scream. You don't have to obey it.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically for identifying and working with protector parts like the fixer.

The grief underneath

I want to be honest about what happens when you stop fixing. You might lose some relationships. Some people in your life are used to you being the rescuer. They might not know what to do with a version of you who says no. Who sets boundaries. Who doesn't drop everything to solve their problems.

That's painful. And it's also information. If a relationship only works when you're depleting yourself, was it really a relationship?

You'll also encounter grief. Deep, old grief. The grief of the child who had to be useful to be seen. The grief of never being loved just for existing. The grief of all the years you spent earning love that should have been given freely. A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work helped me meet that grief with compassion instead of more fixing.

Let yourself feel it. Don't try to fix the grief. Don't try to make it go away. It needs to be witnessed too. By you. For you.

The fixer part doesn't know how to do that. But you can learn. You can learn to be with your own pain the way you've been with everyone else's. You can learn to hold yourself the way you've held others.

This isn't about becoming cold or uncaring. It's about becoming whole. It's about letting the fixer part rest while other parts step forward. The part that can receive. The part that can play. The part that can rest. The part that can be loved without earning it.

Those parts exist in you. They've just been pushed aside by the fixer, who thought it had to do everything alone. It doesn't. You don't.

Wild, right? The one who rescues everyone else might be the one who most needs rescuing. And the rescue doesn't come from outside. It comes from you. From the part of you that can finally say, "You can stop now. I've got this."

Healing Journey Resources

  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
